coredata.dc:title: Chitosan-coated iron oxide/graphene quantum dots as a potential multifunctional nanohybrid for bimodal magnetic resonance/fluorescence imaging and 5-fluorouracil delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Hassani, Samad
Gharehaghaji, Nahideh
Divband, Baharak
coredata.dc:description: Incorporating nanostructures with magnetic and fluorescent features in one nanosystem leads to designing effective multifunctional nanosystems for bimodal imaging-based diagnosis and cancer treatment procedures such as anticancer drug delivery. In this study, the chitosan-coated magnetic iron oxide/graphene quantum dots (MGC) nanohybrid was synthesized and loaded with 5-fluorouracil (MGC-FU) to consider its potential for bimodal magnetic resonance/fluorescence imaging (MRI/FI) and 5-fluorouracil delivery. The nanohybrid was investigated using FT-IR, TEM, and VSM to evaluate physicochemical characteristics, MTT assay to determine A549 cancer cells toxicity, MRI to assess relaxivity, and fluorescence spectrometry to analyze fluorescence properties. In addition, the in vitro loading/release studies of 5-fluorouracil were performed. MGC-FU nanohybrid showed less than 20 nm in size, uniform dispersion, and superparamagnetic behavior. MGC nanohybrid possesses low cytotoxicity, while MGC-FU had the significant cancer cell killing effect even higher than pure 5-fluorouracil. MRI and fluorescence spectroscopy results revealed a high value of transverse relaxivity (r2 = 690 mM−1s−1) and strong fluorescence emission at 493 nm, dependent on the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The findings of in vitro loading/release studies suggested that the MGC nanohybrid could be employed as a nanocarrier for 5-fluorouracil due to the significant drug loading capacity of 90% and pH-dependent sustained release (84% after 48 h, at pH = 5). This study promises to use MGC-FU as a potential multifunctional nanohybrid for bimodal MRI/FI and cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic resonance imaging
Fluorescence imaging
5-fluorouracil
Drug delivery
Nanohybrid

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was derived from the MSc thesis, and it was financially supported by the Medical Radiation Sciences Research Team of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Grant No: 64577 , Ethical code: 1398.442 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids.
TEM image of the as-prepared MGC-FU nanohybrid at high magnification (the inset scale bar is 20 nm).

Magnetization curves of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, MG, MGC, and MGC-FU nanohybrids. Inset shows MGC with a good dispersibility in aqueous solution (sample on the left) and the magnetic separation of MGC nanohybrid after applying an external magnetic field (sample on the right).

MRI study of MG, MGC, and MGC-FU nanohybrids: (a) Gray-scale and color maps of the T2-weighted MR images in various iron concentrations, and (b) 1/T2 relaxation rate versus iron concentration graphs of the nanohybrids.

Fluorescence intensity of MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids (excitation wavelength = 400 nm, emission wavelength = 460–660 nm). Inset: Photograph of MGC-FU nanohybrid solution taken under (a) daylight and (b) ultraviolet light.
Curve of 5-FU release from MGC-FU nanohybrid at pH of 7.4 and 5.

In vitro cytotoxicity of the nanohybrids against A549 alveolar adenocarcinoma cancer cells (a) MG and MGC, and (b) MGC-FU and pure 5-FU. For an accurate comparison, 5-FU concentrations were determined based on the values of the drug loading into MGC after 24 h incubation. All data were reported as the mean ± SD (n = 4). Levels of * P < 0.05 and ** P < 0.01 were considered as significant.

head.ce:title: Chitosan-coated iron oxide/graphene quantum dots as a potential multifunctional nanohybrid for bimodal magnetic resonance/fluorescence imaging and 5-fluorouracil delivery

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Radiology, Faculty of Allied Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Incorporating nanostructures with magnetic and fluorescent features in one nanosystem leads to designing effective multifunctional nanosystems for bimodal imaging-based diagnosis and cancer treatment procedures such as anticancer drug delivery. In this study, the chitosan-coated magnetic iron oxide/graphene quantum dots (MGC) nanohybrid was synthesized and loaded with 5-fluorouracil (MGC-FU) to consider its potential for bimodal magnetic resonance/fluorescence imaging (MRI/FI) and 5-fluorouracil delivery. The nanohybrid was investigated using FT-IR, TEM, and VSM to evaluate physicochemical characteristics, MTT assay to determine A549 cancer cells toxicity, MRI to assess relaxivity, and fluorescence spectrometry to analyze fluorescence properties. In addition, the in vitro loading/release studies of 5-fluorouracil were performed. MGC-FU nanohybrid showed less than 20 nm in size, uniform dispersion, and superparamagnetic behavior. MGC nanohybrid possesses low cytotoxicity, while MGC-FU had the significant cancer cell killing effect even higher than pure 5-fluorouracil. MRI and fluorescence spectroscopy results revealed a high value of transverse relaxivity (r2 = 690 mM−1s−1) and strong fluorescence emission at 493 nm, dependent on the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. The findings of in vitro loading/release studies suggested that the MGC nanohybrid could be employed as a nanocarrier for 5-fluorouracil due to the significant drug loading capacity of 90% and pH-dependent sustained release (84% after 48 h, at pH = 5). This study promises to use MGC-FU as a potential multifunctional nanohybrid for bimodal MRI/FI and cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the design and synthesis of multifunctional nanoprobes have received much attention, providing the basis for the simultaneous diagnosis and treatment of cancer [1]. The presence of components with distinct properties in the structure of multifunctional nanoprobes allows them to provide bimodal or multimodal imaging with therapeutic strategies [2]. The possibility of imaging and drug delivery through a single nanostructure with multiple functions provides a guided therapeutic process and offers numerous benefits, including real-time monitoring of nanocarriers distribution, increasing the drug's effectiveness, and decreasing adverse side effects [3]. Imaging components in the multifunctional nanoplatforms are contrast enhancement agents for various diagnostic modalities such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), fluorescence imaging (FI), computed tomography (CT), and ultrasound imaging (USI) [4]. Despite the advantages of these clinical imaging modalities, each has some drawbacks and cannot provide all necessary diagnostic information. Hence, designing and developing bimodal or multimodal imaging contrast agents to benefit from the advantages of different imaging methods and compensate for their limitations are essential for accurate diagnosis [3]. On the other hand, therapeutic elements in multifunctional nanoprobes include materials such as anticancer drugs for drug delivery, photothermal agents for photothermal therapy (PTT), and photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy (PDT) [5].

Among imaging modalities, MRI is a powerful and noninvasive tool for cancer detection that provides sectional anatomical imaging with high spatial resolution [6]. MRI contrast agents used to compensate for its low sensitivity generally consist of two main categories of T1 and T2 contrast media. These agents shorten the T1 or T2 relaxation times, causing increased signal intensity and brightening the T1-weighted images or decreased signal intensity and darkening the T2-weighted images, respectively [7]. Paramagnetic gadolinium (Gd) chelates are commonly used T1 contrast agents, and superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) are known as T2 contrast materials [8]. Despite providing an appropriate image contrast using Gd chelates, they can deposit in some organs, i.e., the bones and gray matter of the brain. They also can cause nephrogenic systemic fibrosis [9]. Therefore, IONPs with high biocompatibility, outstanding magnetic properties, and the ability to provide T1 contrast seem to be an alternative to Gd-based contrast agents [10]. Besides, the unique physicochemical features of IONPs and their surface modifying properties make them a proper choice for MRI multimodal imaging, monitoring of cancer treatment process, and hyperthermia treatment [11].

FI is the most widespread biological imaging modality because of its ability to extract subcellular level biological information, high sensitivity and specificity, and simplicity [12]. However, FI suffers from low spatial and temporal resolutions and tissue depth penetration [13]. Various nanomaterials such as organic fluorescent dyes, fluorescent proteins, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), and graphene derivatives have been used in FI. Organic dyes are the most commonly used fluorescent nanoprobes. However, their application and fluorescent proteins have been limited due to their low fluorescence stability, low photobleaching resistance, and short fluorescence lifetime. On the other hand, semiconductor QDs due to superior physicochemical characteristics, especially their high stability and feasibility for surface functionalization, are suitable for bio-imaging and tumor targeting and are proper for therapeutic processes such as drug and gene delivery [14].

Fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of the main derivatives of two-dimensional graphene appropriated for cancer imaging. They benefit from very small size, biocompatibility, excellent photostability, extended fluorescence, water dispersibility, easy preparation, and low cost. GQDs are a new generation of fluorophores for FI that provide unique opportunities in bio-imaging for accurate diagnosis [15]. They are preferred over cadmium-based semiconductor QDs such as cadmium selenide (CdSe) and cadmium telluride (CdTe) in biological applications because of their lower toxicity [16].

Combining magnetic and fluorescent properties in one nanostructure leads to fabricating a new type of nanomaterials served for bimodal MRI/FI. Therefore, individual shortcomings of single-mode imaging can be resolved, and anatomical and molecular information can be obtained for a more precise diagnosis [17]. Among magnetic-fluorescent nanostructures, the combinations of Fe3O4/QDs or graphene derivatives have been studied as drug delivery vehicles in addition to their role in bimodal MRI/FI. For example, the researchers have developed the GQD@Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe for carrying doxorubicin (DOX) [18], chitosan encapsulated iron oxide (CdS QDs) multifunctional nanocomposite for carrying podophyllotoxin anticancer drug [19], Fe3O4@La-BTC/GO nanoparticles for loading and controllable release of DOX [20], and GO-Fe3O4 nanoplatform for magnetically targeted DOX delivery [21]. Some studies reported the synthesis of the nanostructures with potential for MRI and 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) drug delivery. For instance, in one study, the multifunctional polycaprolactone (PCL)/chitosan-covered magnetite nanographene oxide loaded with 5-FU was introduced as a nanoplatform for thermo-chemotherapy and the real time monitoring of the tumor response to treatment by MRI [22]. Another study results suggested that the 5-FU-loaded poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid)-coated magnetite nanographene oxide has a great potential for MRI and combined photothermal and chemotherapy [23]. Other studies developed the nanostructures for 5-FU drug delivery without addressing the imaging aspect. The chitosan–functionalized graphene oxide (CS/GO) nanocomposite suggested as a suitable adsorbent for 5-FU loading in the potential drug delivery systems [24] and chitosan-polyacrylic acid encapsulated Fe3O4 magnetic nanogelic core-shell introduced for controlled release of 5-FU [25] are examples of these nanostructures. However, to the best of our knowledge, Fe3O4/GQDs with chitosan coating have not been investigated for 5-FU delivery.

5-FU is one of the most commonly used anticancer drugs with antimetabolite function and effectiveness in treating a wide range of solid tumors such as breast, neck, liver, brain [26], and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) [27]. 5-FU has been used as a drug with meaningful activity in treating colorectal cancer for decades. It is still used in treating colon cancer in modern adjuvant regimens of chemotherapy. Besides, 5-FU shows a great single agent activity among other chemotherapeutic drugs [28]. The hydrophilic property of 5-FU makes it a suitable anticancer drug for physiological environment applications [29]. 5-FU is an analogue of uracil that causes cell death through nucleoside metabolism in RNA and DNA [30]. Although the short half-life of 5-FU in conventional chemotherapy, its non-specific circulation, and its toxic effect on normal cells reduce the drug efficacy and lead to side effects, the problems can be solved using a safe and proper nanosystem for effective delivery of the drug [31].

The present study aimed to develop chitosan-coated Fe3O4-GQDs (MGC) as a multifunctional nanohybrid for MRI/FI and 5-FU delivery. Using chitosan coating, a natural polysaccharide and safe biopolymer, minimizes the nanohybrid's plausible toxicity and increases its biocompatibility [32]. In addition, chitosan-based matrices can provide efficient systems for controlled anticancer drug delivery [33].

Ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) (purity 99%), anhydrous sodium acetate (purity ≥ 99%), citrate acid (purity 99.5%), ethylenediamine (purity>99%), graphite powder (purity>99%), chitosan (molecular weight (MW) = 10 kDa and deacetylation degree (DD) = 92%), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, purity 98%), and N-hydroxysuccinnimide (NHS, purity 98%) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals were analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.

Fe3O4–NH2 magnetic nanoparticles were prepared using the reported method [34]. As a typical reaction, 2.0 g FeCl3·6H2O and 3.5 g anhydrous sodium acetate was added to 180 mL of ethylenediamine in glycol (1:8) and stirred vigorously at room temperature to give a transparent solution. Then the solution was transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (300 mL) and heated (200 °C, 8 h). The Fe3O4–NH2 nanoparticles were collected using a magnet and rinsed three times with ethanol and distilled water to remove the solvent and unbound ethanediamine, and then dried at 60 °C.

For the preparation of Fe3O4–GQDs composites, 0.5 g Fe3O4–NH2 magnetic nanoparticles were dispersed in distilled water with ultrasonic mixing (10 min), then citrate acid (0.9 g) and ethylenediamine (1 mL) were added to the suspension and mixed for 1 h. The mixture was transferred into an autoclave (100 mL) and heated (200 °C, 4 h). The solid product was purified in a dialysis bag (MWCO, 1000 Da) against 500 mL water (12 h, three times), freeze-dried at −45 °C for 24 h, and named MG.

For the synthesis of chitosan-coated Fe3O4-GQDs nanocomposite, chitosan powder (50 mg) was dissolved in acetic acid solution (2.5 mL; 1% (w/v)) with magnetic stirring and then followed by the addition of MG suspension. Next, to adjust the pH at 7 and gelation of chitosan, NaOH (0.1 M) was added dropwise to the mixture and stirred for 24 h. After all, MG wrapped with chitosan gels is purified by dialysis (MWCO, 1000 Da) in 200 mL water for 12 h (repeated for three times), then freeze-dried at −45 °C for 24 h and named as MGC.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids were recorded by a Bomem MB-Series FT-IR spectrophotometer (Hartman–Braun, USA) with a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using KBr pellets. The size and shape of MGC-FU nanohybrid were assessed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM: Zeiss LEO 912 Omega), using an accelerating voltage of 140 kV. Investigation of the magnetic properties of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, MG, MGC, and MGC-FU nanohybrids was performed on a VSM-7400 vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at room temperature. The curves of magnetization versus applied magnetic field (−14,000 ≤ H ≤ +14,000 G) were plotted.

Various Fe concentrations (0.01, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 mM) of the nanohybrids were dispersed in a 2% agar aqueous solution and decanted into glass tubes (10 mL) and 2% agar aqueous solution was selected as the control sample (zero concentration). The samples were regularly placed inside a plastic rack located in the center of the head coil. MRI was carried out using a 1.5 T clinical system (Avanto, Siemens, Germany). The T2-weighted MR images were acquired via a multi-echo spin echo pulse sequence in the coronal plane. The imaging parameters were as follow: TR = 3500 ms, TE values from 14 to 224 ms at intervals of 14 ms, slice thickness = 5 mm, matrix size = 256 × 256, field of view (FOV) = 200 × 200 mm2, and number of excitations (NEX) = 1 [35].

The MR images with DICOM format were transferred to the RadiAnt DICOM Viewer (64-bit) software to measure the signal intensities of certain areas in selected sections. By transitioning the resulting data in the MATLAB curve-fitting toolbox, the exponential T2 curves were gained, and the relaxation times were determined. For the r2 relaxometry, the graph of the 1/T2 relaxation rate versus Fe concentration was plotted for each nanohybrid. A line was created from fitting the obtained points that its slope was equal to the value of r2 relaxivity.

Fluorescent analyses of MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids were performed by a monochromator-based fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer LS 55, USA). Fluorescence measurements were assessed with excitation wavelength = 400 nm and emission wavelength = 460–660 nm at room temperature and constant pH of 7.

5-FU was loaded on MGC based on the previously reported strategy with some corrections [31]. 50 mg of MGC was dispersed in 20 mL of PBS with a pH of 7.4 and ultrasounded for 10 min. At the next step, 10 mL of 5-FU solution with 2 mg/mL concentration was added to the final dispersion solution. The mixture was stirred (400 rpm) for 8 h at room temperature in the dark. Centrifugation was carried out to assemble 5-FU loaded nanoparticles, and then MGC-FU nanohybrid was washed with deionized water to remove the free 5-FU. The drug loading capacity was calculated according to the previous study [36].

The release behavior of 5-FU from MGC-FU nanohybrid was investigated based on the reported procedure [31]. 20 mg of MGC-FU nanohybrid was dispersed in 10 mL of PBS containing 0.1% w/v Tween-80 at pH 7.4 and 5. Tween-80 acts as an emulsifier to prevent the sedimentation of the released drug [37]. The mixture was stirred at 500 rpm in the dark. At predetermined time intervals, 1 mL of the supernatant was removed through centrifugation (5000 rpm) for 5 min and replaced with 1 mL of fresh PBS. The amount of released 5-FU in each sample was determined using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 480 nm wavelength considering the peak area associated with the 5-FU standard calibration curve in a similar buffer solution and average ± SD (n = 4) experiment. The 5-FU drug release rate was calculated using the following equation:
W =
C
ti
× V +
∑
C
ti

V

C

100
Where W is the drug release rate; C
ti is the concentration of 5-FU in the drawing PBS at t
i (mg mL−1); V
ti is the volume of drawing PBS at t
i ; C 0 and V 0 are the initial concentration (mg mL−1) and volume of 5-FU solution; C 1 and V 1 are the concentration (mg mL−1) and volume of the 5-FU supernatant by centrifuging after the drug loading [38].

The cytotoxicity test of MG, MGC, MGC-FU nanohybrids, and pure 5-FU was performed using MTT (3-(4, 5-dimetylthiazol- 2-yl)− 2, 5-diphenyl- trazolium bromide) assay. A549 alveolar adenocarcinoma cells were seeded in a 96-well cell culture plate at a density of 4 × 104 cells/well, and then incubated in a humidified atmosphere at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were exposed to different concentrations of MG, MGC, MGC-FU, and pure 5-FU and were incubated again for 24 h. The used media were discarded, and the wells were washed with PBS. In the next step, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well, followed by further incubation for 3 h. Afterward, the MTT solution was removed, and 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was poured into each well. At last, the optical absorbance of the experimental and control groups was determined by a microplate reader spectrophotometer (ELISA plate reader: BioTek, Bad Friedrichshall, Germany) at 570 nm.

The statistical analysis of the data was carried out using GraphPad Prism 8.0.2 software, and the results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical significance was analyzed by two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey correction. Levels of *P < 0.05 and ** P < 0.01 were considered as statistically significant.

The FT-IR spectra of MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids are shown in Fig. 1. FT-IR spectrum of MGC indicated the peaks at 478 and 540 cm−1 was assigned to the FeO bond vibration of Fe3O4, 1535 cm−1, and 1388 cm−1 (amide II band, CN stretching peak), 1110 cm−1 (COC bending vibration) of chitosan, 1643 cm−1 (CO) of GQDs, 2863 cm−1 and 2098 cm−1 (CH) of ethylenediamine, 3263 cm−1 and 3417 cm−1 corresponded to N–H and OH bond vibration, respectively. In the FT-IR spectrum of MGC-FU, the shift in peaks of CO, CN, CO, and OH to lower wavenumbers (1064 cm−1, 1326 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, and 3386 cm−1) indicated that 5-FU was loaded successfully in the nanohybrid.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM image of MGC-FU nanohybrid. The image indicates chitosan as a thin sheet. The large dark spots on chitosan represent the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and the small dark spots surrounding Fe3O4 nanoparticles’ surface demonstrate GQDs, confirming successful synthesizing of GQDs on the Fe3O4 nanoparticles. As seen in the Figure, the morphology of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles was spherical. The spherical Fe3O4 nanoparticles and GQDs together had an average size of 15 nm. They were well-dispersed within the chitosan without distinguished agglomeration. That is to say, the agglomeration of GQDs can be effectively inhibited by the chitosan gels, resulting in enhanced fluorescent stability of the MGC-FU nanohybrid.

The measured size of the nanostructures is important in terms of physiologic considerations. Since the effective pore size of the normal vascular endothelial layer is about 5 nm, nanostructures with a size smaller than 5 nm, are transported into the extracellular space rapidly. Thus, they experience shorter circulation time in the blood. Additionally, nanostructures with a size less than 8 nm may be undergone glomerular filtration and rapidly excreted by the renal system [39]. Both of these situations limit the imaging time. Since MGC-FU nanohybrid has larger size than these thresholds, it is expected that it can provide enough imaging time for future intravenous studies.

The field-dependent magnetization measurement of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles and all nanohybrids are illustrated in Fig. 3. The absence of hysteresis cycles, coercivity, or residual magnetization reflects the superparamagnetic feature of all samples. The saturation magnetization (Ms) of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles was 82 emu/g, while MG showed Ms of 74.4 emu/g. This reduction in Ms value is attributed to the disordering of the magnetic moment after placing the GQDs on the Fe3O4 cores. The Ms was decreased to 72.3 emu/g in MGC due to the shielding effect of the polymer layer of chitosan. Finally, by the presence of 5-FU, Ms of MGC-FU reached 67.2 emu/g. Comparing these results with other studies showed their lower values of Ms, e.g., 50.4 emu/g for the Fe3O4/Graphene [40], and 43.23 emu/g for the Fe3O4–PEG–GO nanocomposites [41]. Despite the presence of Fe3O4, chitosan, and GQDs in the Fe3O4@CS-GQDs nanocomposite synthesized in the Wang et al. study, they reported an Ms value of 11.4 emu/g that is more than 6 times lower than MGC. This is due to the direct attachment of chitosan on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in their study that reduced the mass fraction of Fe3O4 in the nanocomposite [42]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the magnetic response can be mainly attributed to the coverage configuration of the Fe3O4 core by other components.
The reduction of the Ms in MG, MGC, and MGC-FU also indirectly suggests the successful surface functionalization of the nanohybrids.

The inset of Fig. 3 demonstrates two samples of MGC nanohybrid. The color sample on the left shows the nanohybrid with a good dispersibility in aqueous solution, resulting in a homogeneous suspension. This is due to the superparamagnetic behavior of MGC that facilitates the nanohybrid dispersion in the solution and prevents magnetic clustering. When the external magnetic field was applied (sample on the right), the MGCs were rapidly separated from the aqueous solution, and a colorless solution was obtained. This finding confirms that MGC can be manipulated by an external magnetic field, indicating the nanohybrid potential to participate in the magnetic targeted drug delivery system.

Fig. 4a illustrates the T2-weighted images and color maps related to MG, MGC, and MGC-FU nanohybrids. The gradual darkening of the T2-weighted images shows a reduction of the signal intensity in a Fe concentration dependent manner. This trend can also be seen in the color map images that show color changes from orange to blue with increasing Fe concentration. Although the signal reduction of the images was seen for all samples, it was higher for MG. This finding can be explained by the chitosan and 5-FU presence on the surface of MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids. The Fe3O4 nanoparticles in the nanohybrids create local magnetic field inhomogeneities and enhance protons' dephasing. In MG, the nanoparticles access many water protons, leading to significant spin-spin interactions and a subsequently strong reduction in the T2 relaxation times and darkness of the images. On the other hand, in MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids, chitosan coating and additional 5-FU drug occupy the space around the Fe3O4 cores (the area with the most field fluctuations), increasing the distance between the magnetic cores and the surrounding water molecules. Therefore, the region influenced by the magnetic inhomogeneity and effective water residency is reduced. Consequently, T2 shortening is weakened, leading to less darkness of the images, especially for MGC-FU nanohybrid.

The r2 relaxivity of the nanohybrids was calculated from the linear fitting between the corresponding relaxation rates and concentrations of Fe ions (Fig. 4b). Increasing in the 1/T2 values as a function of the Fe ions concentration was seen for all samples. MG with the lowest T2 relaxation time, and therefore, the highest 1/T2 showed the greatest r2 relaxivity. The r2 values for MGC and MGC-FU nanohybrids were significantly lower than MG due to the distance between the water protons and the magnetic cores that reduces the magnetic inhomogeneity. However, their great transverse relaxivity values confirm the high potential of these nanohybrids to produce negative contrast in MRI. The r2 relaxivity of MGC-FU nanohybrid (690 mM−1s−1) is significantly higher than the commercial superparamagnetic iron oxide-based contrast agents such as Endorem (r2 ~ 41 mM−1s−1) and Resovist (r2 ~ 61 mM−1s−1) at 1.5 T [43]. In addition, MGC-FU showed superior r2 relaxivity compared to the GO-Fe3O4 nanocomposite as a drug delivery platform for DOX [21]. It should be noted that the r2 value of MGC nanohybrid is considerably more significant than the magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs)/GO/chitosan nanosystem [44]. Also, MG showed the r2 relaxivity higher than that of the GO chitosan-Fe3O4 [45] and magnetic graphene oxide (MGO) nanocomposites [46]. However, differences in the synthesis methods, iron oxide core size, coating materials thickness and type lead to different r2 relaxivities among the studies.

It should be noted that the blood half-life of an MRI nano contrast agent depends on its size [47]. The nanohybrid size in this study is larger than the threshold of the glomerular filtration of the renal system. Therefore, a prolonged blood circulation time in the future potential in vivo studies may be provided, leading to enough imaging time to produce effective contrast in MRI and improve the image quality.

The fluorescence spectra indicate the optical properties of the nanohybrids. The strongest emission peaks of MGC and MGC-FU representing their superior energy gap were seen at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm ( Fig. 5). MGC and MGC-FU showed stable emission peaks at 492 and 493 nm, respectively, corresponding to the blue region of the visible light spectrum as indicated in the bottom of Fig. 5 with the arrow. The inset shows fluorescence color changes of MGC-FU after exposure to ultraviolet light. Adding 5-FU to the MGC showed no evident emission wavelength shift, but the fluorescent signal intensity was increased significantly. This finding may be due to displaying the fluorescent property of the 5-FU after exposure to UV–vis light at wavelength ranges of 400–600 nm [48]. Encapsulation of GQDs within the chitosan biopolymer gels which occurs by dehydration of the amino groups of chitosan with carboxyl groups on the GQDs can preserve the photoluminescence properties of GQDs for a long time. This means that, the fluorescent signal intensity does not decrease over time. This is further confirmation of the nanohybrid potential as a nanoprobe for FI.
Since the fluorescence intensity and emission peaks of GQDs and their compounds are changed in various pHs, the fluorescent measurements were performed under the constant pH condition (pH = 7) [21].

Compared to other studies, Xu et al. obtained the wavelength values close to our results for Fe3O4@La-BTC/GO-PEG nanoparticles (excitation wavelength = 400 nm, emission wavelength = 550 nm) [20]. Wang et al. exhibited an emission peak at 434 nm under the excitation light of 330 nm for Fe3O4 @CS-GQDs nanocomposite [42]. In another study, excitation and emission wavelengths of the reduced graphene oxide–iron oxide quantum dots were reported at 320 and 398 nm, respectively [49]. Differences in the pH values, photoluminescence properties of the components participating in the nanostructures, and employed excitation wavelengths lead to different emission wavelengths among the studies [50].

In this study drug (5-FU) carrier ability of MGC nanohybrid was investigated. The results demonstrated that the loading amount of the drug reached 90% of the drug content in the 5-FU solution within 8 h. Most of the drug amount was loaded in the chitosan chains. The presence of the functional groups, i.e., amino and carboxyl in chitosan, makes it a degradable biomaterial and increases its ability to attach to the anticancer drug. The addition of chitosan to the MG structure paves the way for the fabrication of a nanocarrier that can be used to deliver anticancer drugs in a controlled manner to reduce side effects and increases therapeutic efficacy [51]. In the sites without chitosan, the drug molecules were bonded to GQDs sheets via hydrogen bonding and π–π stacking due to their aromatic structure. This can be an important factor in the high loading capacity of the drug [52].

Fig. 6 shows the release profile of 5-FU loaded MGC nanohybrid in similar conditions to physiological fluid (pH 7.4) and a tumoral environment (pH 5) at 37 °C. According to the drug release profile, the initial burst releases of 5-FU were observed at two different pH values so that at the first 10 h, 57% and 70% of the loaded drug were released at pH 7.4 and 5, respectively. Then, a sustained release was begun that was reached 69.3% and 84% at 48 h, when tested at the pH 7.4 and 5, respectively. The results verify the existence of a pH-dependent drug release pattern. A higher release rate in the acidic condition compared to the normal one can be due to the variation of hydrogen bonding interaction between MGC and 5-FU and increasing the solubility of chitosan. Chitosan can undergo a reversible swelling/deswelling process due to amino groups in its chains and contribute to the pH-dependent drug release mechanism [53]. The pH-dependent drug release can enhance the effectiveness of cancer treatment by selectively increasing drug release in tumoral environments that possess low pH [54]. In other words, the side effects are reduced by reducing the leakage of the anticancer drug in the region of normal tissues. Besides, the employment of chitosan-based nanocarrier can eliminate the short half-life problem of 5-FU in clinical use, reducing the side effects of repeated drug administration. The released drug amount in this study is higher than that of the 5-FU loaded Fe3O4@chitosan-PEG nanoparticles with 27% at pH 7.4% and 52% at pH 5.8 after 48 h [55]. These differences in the drug release may be due to the different sizes of the nanostructures. Since our synthesized nanohybrid is smaller than Fe3O4@chitosan-PEG with 95 nm size, its surface area in contact with the dissolution medium is increased, leading to a more significant amount of the drug release.

The cytocompatibility of the nanohybrids and the effect of pure 5-FU on A549 alveolar adenocarcinoma cancer cells were investigated via MTT assay. MG and MGC exhibited a concentration-dependent increase in cytotoxicity; however, even at the highest concentration, the survival rate remained above 75% ( Fig. 7a). MGC showed greater cytocompatibility than MG in different concentrations due to the presence of chitosan as a safe biopolymer. On the other hand, according to Fig. 7b, the cytotoxicity of MGC was increased after loading with 5-FU, confirming successful encapsulation of 5-FU inside MGC nanohybrid. MGC-FU demonstrated higher cytotoxicity than pure 5-FU at all concentrations. For instance, at the highest concentration of 18.7 μg/mL, the cytotoxicity of MGC-FU and pure 5-FU were 94% and 80%, respectively. The cytotoxicity reached 90% at the concentration of 9.3 μg/mL for MGC-FU nanohybrid, while to achieve a similar result with pure 5-FU, a 2-fold higher concentration was required. Therefore, MGC nanohybrid, besides being a suitable carrier for 5-FU, can also succeed in the death of A549 alveolar adenocarcinoma cancer cells after combining with 5-FU.

This study developed the chitosan-coated Fe3O4-GQDs (MGC) nanohybrid with 5-FU loading (MGC-FU) as a multifunctional nanosystem for bimodal MRI/FI and 5-FU delivery. The VSM results revealed that the nanohybrid had superparamagnetic characteristic. Rapid separation of MGC from the aqueous solution by an external magnetic field confirmed the nanohybrid potential to participate in the magnetic targeted drug delivery system. MRI measurements demonstrated a high r2 relaxivity for MGC-FU, confirming its potential as a negative contrast agent for MRI. Based on the fluorescence study, MGC-FU nanohybrid emitted a stable and strong photoluminescence peak at the visible light range, confirming its ability to use for fluorescence imaging. The results of the MTT assay showed low cytotoxicity of MGC and high cancer cell toxicity of MGC-FU nanohybrids. An efficient drug loading capacity and a sustained release in a pH-dependent manner demonstrated that MGC-FU could apply for cancer therapy. Moreover, the 5-FU therapeutic efficacy was enhanced by conjugation with MGC nanohybrid due to the chitosan presence, which paves the way for nanocarrier fabrication. Therefore, the pH-responsive MGC-FU nanohybrid is a promising candidate for bimodal MRI/FI and targeted 5-FU delivery.

According to the outstanding magnetic properties of MGC-FU, it can participate in the process of magnetically targeted drug delivery and hyperthermal therapy. This can pave the way to develop new therapeutic applications of MGC-FU nanohybrid for cancer treatment in future studies.

Samad Hassani: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft preparation. Nahideh Gharehaghaji: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Baharak Divband: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was derived from the MSc thesis, and it was financially supported by the Medical Radiation Sciences Research Team of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (Grant No: 64577, Ethical code: 1398.442).
